stem: A 15-month-old child is brought for a well-child visit. The parents note he has 3 words, walks independently, and uses a pincer grasp. However, he does not point to objects of interest, does not respond to his name consistently, and has poor eye contact. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Reassure parents — all milestones are normal
Refer for formal autism spectrum disorder screening

explanation:
Correct Answer: Red flags for ASD at 12-18 months: no pointing (protodeclarative), poor eye contact, not responding to name, no shared attention (joint attention deficits). Motor and language milestones may be on track. AAP recommends universal ASD screening at 18 and 24 months (M-CHAT-R/F), but concerns at any age warrant immediate referral. Early intervention (before age 3) significantly improves outcomes.

Evidence: Developmental Milestones - StatPearls - Palpallatoc EM, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK557518. Updated 2024.

choices.text: Blood culture, CBC, UA, LP, and empiric antibiotics

explanation:
Correct Answer: Neonatal sepsis <28 days: full workup + empiric ampicillin/gentamicin. Do NOT wait for results.

stem: 3yo boy, 6-day fever 40C, bilateral conjunctival injection, cracked lips, strawberry tongue, 2cm cervical LN, polymorphous rash, irritable.Vital SignsBP 100/60HR 140RR 28Temp 104FSpO₂ 98% Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Kawasaki disease requires fever >=5 days + >=4/5 criteria: conjunctivitis, rash, adenopathy, oral changes, extremity changes.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- CRASH criteria met
- Treat within 10 days to prevent coronary aneurysms
- IVIG 2g/kg + high-dose aspirin
- Echo at diagnosis, 2wk, 6-8wk

Evidence: Kawasaki Disease - StatPearls - McCrindle BW, et al. NBK537163. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Scarlet fever: Sandpaper rash, pharyngitis, responds to antibiotics.
- Measles: Cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, Koplik spots.
- JIA: Chronic arthritis; systemic JIA has quotidian fevers.
- TSS: Hypotension, desquamation, multi-organ.

Reference: Kawasaki Disease - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537163/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 9mo boy with episodic crying, draws legs up, lethargy between episodes, currant jelly stools, sausage-shaped RUQ mass.Vital SignsBP 90/55HR 150RR 32Temp 100.2FSpO₂ 99% Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain, currant jelly stools, sausage-shaped mass. Most common bowel obstruction in children 6mo-3yr.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Classic triad (in <50% of cases)
- US: target sign/pseudokidney 95% sensitive
- Air/hydrostatic enema: diagnostic AND therapeutic 80-95%
- Lead point rare 3yr

Evidence: Intussusception - StatPearls - Marsicovetere P, et al. NBK431078. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Pyloric stenosis: Projectile non-bilious vomiting in 3-6wk infant.
- Meckel: Painless rectal bleeding, not episodic pain with mass.
- Appendicitis: Periumbilical to RLQ pain, no currant jelly.
- Hirschsprung: Chronic constipation from birth.

Reference: Intussusception - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431078/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 2yo boy, 2 days URI, now barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice. Worse at night. No drooling.Vital SignsBP 98/58HR 120RR 28Temp 100.8FSpO₂ 96% Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Croup presents in children 6mo-3yr with barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness, and URI prodrome. Steeple sign on AP neck X-ray.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Parainfluenza (75%) most common cause
- No drooling differentiates from epiglottitis
- Single dose dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg effective for all severity
- Nebulized racemic epinephrine for severe stridor

Evidence: Croup - StatPearls - Smith DK, et al. NBK431070. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Epiglottitis: Rapid onset, drooling, tripod position, toxic, thumb sign.
- Foreign body: Sudden onset without URI prodrome.
- Bacterial tracheitis: High fever, toxic, poor response to croup Rx.
- PTA: Older children, trismus, uvular deviation.

Reference: Croup - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431070/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 3-day-old full-term breastfed infant jaundiced to umbilicus. Mom O+, baby A+. Direct Coombs positive.Vital SignsBP 80/50HR 140RR 40Temp 98.6FSpO₂ 99% Most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Total/direct bilirubin, CBC with retics, plot on Bhutani nomogram

explanation:
Correct Answer: Jaundice with positive Coombs (ABO incompatibility) requires total/direct bilirubin, CBC with retics, and Bhutani nomogram plotting.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Positive Coombs = hemolytic disease
- Bhutani: hour-specific bilirubin determines risk zone
- ABO incompatibility (O mother, A/B baby) causes hemolysis
- Phototherapy thresholds based on GA, risk factors, hour-specific bili

Evidence: Neonatal Jaundice - StatPearls - Muchowski KE. NBK532930. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Reassurance: Positive Coombs requires bilirubin measurement.
- Abdominal US: For conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, not hemolytic.
- Exchange transfusion: Premature without knowing bilirubin level.
- Formula and recheck: Doesn't address hemolytic process.

Reference: Neonatal Jaundice - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532930/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 18mo previously healthy boy, 2-min GTC seizure during 103F fever from otitis media. Returned to baseline in 10 min. No prior seizures.Vital SignsBP 98/60HR 130RR 28Temp 103FSpO₂ 98% Most appropriate workup?

choices.text: Treat underlying infection and reassure; no neurologic workup needed

explanation:
Correct Answer: A simple febrile seizure (generalized, <15 min, single in 24h, age 6mo-5yr, return to baseline) requires no EEG, imaging, or AEDs per AAP guidelines.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- AAP: no routine EEG/imaging/labs for simple febrile seizure
- LP only if meningeal signs or unimmunized
- Does NOT increase epilepsy risk significantly
- Recurrence ~30% but doesn't warrant prophylaxis

Evidence: Febrile Seizures - StatPearls - Leung AKC, et al. NBK448204. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- EEG and MRI: Not indicated per AAP guidelines.
- LP: Not needed without meningeal signs in immunized child >12mo.
- Start AED: Not recommended for simple febrile seizures.
- CT head: No imaging indicated.

Reference: Febrile Seizures - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448204/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 4-week-old firstborn male, projectile non-bilious vomiting after feeds x5 days. Hungry after vomiting. Weight loss. Olive-shaped epigastric mass.Vital SignsBP 80/50HR 160RR 36Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 99% Most appropriate diagnostic test?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Abdominal ultrasound is gold standard for pyloric stenosis. Muscle thickness >3mm and channel length >15mm diagnostic. Check electrolytes (hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- US >95% sensitivity
- Criteria: muscle >3mm, channel >15mm
- Classic: hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
- Pyloromyotomy after electrolyte correction

Evidence: Pyloric Stenosis - StatPearls - Ranells JD, et al. NBK442019. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- UGI series: Second-line if US equivocal.
- AXR: May show dilated stomach but not diagnostic.
- CT: Unnecessary radiation; US sufficient.
- Electrolytes only: Essential but need US for anatomic diagnosis.

Reference: Pyloric Stenosis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK442019/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 6yo boy, palpable purpura on buttocks/lower extremities, colicky abdominal pain, bilateral knee/ankle arthritis. UA: 2+ blood, 1+ protein.Vital SignsBP 100/64HR 96RR 18Temp 99.0FSpO₂ 98% Which monitoring is most important?

choices.text: Serial UA, urine protein/creatinine ratio, BP monitoring

explanation:
Correct Answer: In IgA vasculitis (HSP), renal monitoring (serial UA, UPCR, BP) for at least 6 months is critical. Nephritis determines long-term prognosis.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Renal involvement in 20-50%
- Nephritis can develop weeks-months after onset
- Monitor UA weekly x1mo, monthly x6mo
- Renal biopsy if nephrotic-range proteinuria

Evidence: IgA Vasculitis - StatPearls - Piram M, et al. NBK537252. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Skin biopsy: Confirms diagnosis but doesn't address prognostic concern (renal).
- ANA: Lupus workup; HSP has IgA-mediated mechanism.
- Bone marrow: Platelets normal in HSP; purpura is vasculitic.
- Coagulation: Normal in HSP; vasculitic, not coagulopathic.

Reference: IgA Vasculitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537252/ [Accessed 2025]
